author: [{'author': 'The name of the individual who authored the academic work, presented as centered text in a visually distinct, formal style (often all caps or title case) on a title page or abstract page, immediately following an attribution phrase like "by". The text must be the sole named individual in its immediate vicinity, without any other names or titles appearing on the same line or in a directly associated block immediately following it. It must not appear within any structured data presentation element such as a table, figure, or list, nor within the body of the text, a bibliographic reference list, a heading, subheading, or section title, or a copyright notice. It is the primary identifier of the work’s creator and must be positioned in a centered block, typically separated from surrounding text by significant whitespace. Furthermore, it must be the only named individual appearing in the immediate context of the "By" or equivalent attribution phrase, and must not be part of a committee list, advisory group, or any other collective attribution. Additionally, it must not be part of a title or heading, and must not be immediately followed by a list of committee members or advisors.'}]
chapter: A major division of the document, often labeled with a number and title.
equation: Symbols and numbers arranged to represent a mathematical concept.
equation number: [{'equation number': 'A numerical identifier, typically enclosed in parentheses or brackets, positioned immediately to the right of a mathematical formula with a clear visual separation. This identifier is not part of the mathematical expression itself but serves as a distinct reference marker for the equation within the document, functioning as a label for referencing the equation rather than a component of the mathematical relationship. It is exclusively found in contexts of mathematical or scientific notation, and is never embedded within code or programming logic, nor is it used as a footnote marker or citation reference within the main text body. Furthermore, it is never part of a list of figures, tables, or other document structural elements, and is always associated with a mathematical expression rather than a descriptive caption or figure title. Additionally, it is typically rendered in a distinct typographic style, such as being bolded or italicized, to further differentiate it from surrounding text. Crucially, it is not found within a table or any other tabular structure, as it is exclusively associated with mathematical expressions outside of such layouts. It is typically associated with a complex mathematical expression that is part of a discussion of derivation or application, and is positioned immediately after such an expression. The identifier must be a pure numerical value or a combination of a numerical value and a letter, and must not be preceded by any text such as "equation" or "Eq.".'}]
figure: [{'figure': 'A graphical illustration or plot that visually represents measured, calculated, or simulated data or a physical structure, designed to provide visual context for the surrounding text and to convey information that is not presented in tabular, textual, or list form, and which is explicitly labeled with a caption and figure number to indicate its role as a standalone visual element within a document.'}]
figure caption: [{'figure caption': 'A self-contained block of text that serves as a descriptive label for a preceding visual figure, positioned immediately after it and visually separated from the surrounding text. It must include a figure identifier (e.g., "Figure 2.8" or "Figure 38") and provide a clear, explicit description of the figure’s content, components, or context, enabling the reader to understand the figure without external reference. It is not part of the main body of text, nor is it a structural heading, a reference to a future figure, or a bibliographic entry. Crucially, it must be directly associated with a preceding visual element (like a chart, graph, or diagram) and function as its explicit, standalone explanation. Furthermore, it must be located at the end of a figure’s visual content and not embedded within or interrupting the flow of the main text or other structural elements like section headings. Additionally, it must be positioned such that it is not itself a visual element (like a table or chart) and must be clearly distinct from the data it describes, serving only as an explanatory label. It must also be visually and semantically subordinate to the figure it describes, appearing as a direct annotation or label rather than a primary structural component of the document layout. Importantly, it must not be a structural navigation element such as a table of contents, chapter heading, or section index, which are designed to guide the reader through the document’s structure rather than explain a specific visual element. Furthermore, it must not be a title or heading for a document or section, as these serve a different structural and informational purpose within the document hierarchy. The text must be formatted as a single, continuous paragraph or a series of short, closely spaced sentences, and must not contain any visual elements such as charts, graphs, or images. Additionally, it must not be a heading or label for a table, as tables are distinct structural elements that contain data and are not merely explanatory labels for preceding content.'}]
footnote: [{'footnote': 'A textual block positioned at the bottom of a page, visually separated from the main text by a horizontal line, and directly linked to preceding text via a superscript marker. It is formatted with a smaller font size and contains explanatory, supplementary, or citation-based content that provides additional context, examples, or references supporting the preceding text. The content is a continuous block of text, not structured as a table, list, or caption, and is not part of the main body or headings. Crucially, it must be explicitly referenced by a superscript number or symbol within the main text and must be located immediately following the reference point, not as a standalone caption or figure label.'}]
list of content heading: [{'list of content heading': 'A structural heading in a document that serves as an index for tables, explicitly labeled with text such as "List of Tables" or a direct, unambiguous equivalent indicating its function. It is positioned at the top of a page and immediately precedes a structured list of entries, each containing a table identifier and its corresponding page number, with consistent spacing or alignment. The entries are exclusively table-related and do not include figures, equations, or general text. The heading is visually distinct from surrounding content and is not followed by explanatory paragraphs, tables, or reference-style entries. It is functionally and structurally separate from chapter titles, section headings, lists of figures, or bibliographic lists.'}]
list of content text: [{'list of content text': 'A vertically aligned sequence of entries, each consisting of a numerical identifier prefixed by either "Table" or "Figure", followed by a descriptive title or caption and a page number, serving as a navigational guide to locate specific figures or tables within a document, where the entries are consistently formatted with a uniform structure and are typically found in a dedicated "List of Figures" or "List of Tables" section.'}]
page number: [{'page number': 'A standalone numeral that functions solely as a sequential identifier for the page position within a document, visually and semantically detached from any structural or labeled document elements such as headings, captions, lists, tables, figures, footnotes, bibliographic entries, or metadata. It appears independently, without integration into any content or layout structure, and is typically positioned at the periphery of the page, serving exclusively to denote page order rather than to convey information, reference, or structure.'}]
paragraph: [{'paragraph': 'A self-contained unit of written prose composed of multiple sentences that form a coherent thought or argument, presented in a continuous block with consistent line breaks and margins, conveying a complete idea or narrative segment through flowing, integrated text, and visually distinct from structural elements such as titles, headings, or section labels, which are typically emphasized through formatting (e.g., bold, larger font, centered alignment) and serve as organizational identifiers rather than narrative exposition. The unit must not exhibit any structural formatting that marks it as a heading or section identifier, nor should it be embedded within a tabular layout, list-based structure, or any other non-continuous, grid-based presentation format, and must be surrounded by white space or other non-textual elements that clearly separate it from adjacent tabular or list-based content. Additionally, it must not be visually emphasized through bolding, centering, or other formatting that would identify it as a heading. Furthermore, it must not be positioned directly beneath a figure or chart in a way that it serves as a caption or descriptive label for that visual element, even if it is formatted as text. It must also not be part of a list, table, or any other itemized or grid-based structure, as such structures are inherently non-continuous and do not represent a single, flowing narrative unit. Crucially, it must not be immediately preceded by a figure or diagram that is visually distinct and serves as a primary visual element, as the text following such a figure is typically a caption or description rather than a standalone paragraph of narrative prose. Moreover, it must not be located directly below a figure or chart where its content is explicitly describing or labeling the visual data presented above it, as such text functions as a caption rather than a narrative paragraph. Additionally, it must not be immediately following a figure or diagram that is visually distinct and serves as a primary visual element, as the text following such a figure is typically a caption or description rather than a standalone paragraph of narrative prose.'}]
reference text: [{'reference text': 'A structured, continuous list of bibliographic entries, each containing complete publication details and presented in a uniform, sequential format, serving as a formal record of external sources cited in a document, distinct from inline notes or marginal annotations.'}]
section: [{'section': 'A visually emphasized heading, typically enclosed in a distinct rectangular frame, that introduces a new, subordinate topic or subtopic within a larger document, immediately followed by a continuous body of explanatory text that elaborates on the introduced topic. The heading is distinct from data tables, captions, or reference markers, and functions as a structural marker for a hierarchical break in the document, embedded within the main narrative flow. It must be visually separated from the surrounding text and not appear as part of a continuous paragraph. The heading often includes a numerical identifier and a descriptive title, and is positioned at the start of a new block of content that is thematically or structurally distinct from the preceding text, and is not part of a list or index structure.'}]
subsection: Secondary subheadings under sections. This is typically represented using a number followed by a decimal point, followed by another number (e.g. Section 3.2).
subsubsection: Specific subdivisions within subsections. This is typically represented using a number followed by a decimal point, followed by a number, followed by a decimal point, followed by another number (e.g. Section 3.2.4).
table: [{'table': 'A structured grid of rows and columns containing quantitative or categorical data, organized under headers, used to present measurable information or results from analysis, characterized by consistent alignment, clear cell boundaries, and a focus on numerical or statistical values for comparison and analysis, excluding hierarchical lists or textual outlines.'}]
table caption: [{'table caption': 'A concise, standalone label that is directly and explicitly associated with a specific table, appearing immediately above or below it to identify or summarize its content. It is typically formatted in a distinct, bolded, or capitalized style to stand out from surrounding text and is functionally tied to the tabular data it describes, serving as a clear identifier for that specific table. It must not be a mathematical formula, a footnote, a source attribution, or a statistical annotation, but rather a descriptive heading that labels the table itself and is positioned in close proximity to the table’s header or boundary, not embedded within or adjacent to a mathematical equation or its label, nor should it be a standalone equation number or reference marker. It is visually and contextually distinct from footnotes, which are positioned at the end of paragraphs or sections and provide supplementary information rather than labeling a table. Additionally, it must not be a general heading for a list of tables or a document-level section title, as these serve a broader organizational purpose rather than labeling an individual table. Furthermore, it must not be part of a list of section entries or a table of contents, which are structured as itemized lists with page numbers and serve navigational purposes rather than labeling a specific table. Crucially, it must not be a standalone page number or a figure/figure caption that is not directly associated with a table, but rather a label that is intrinsically linked to the table’s structure and content. It must also not be a caption for a figure or diagram, as its function is exclusively to label a table, not to describe a visual element or illustration. Furthermore, it must not be a bibliographic reference list or a collection of citations, as these are structured as a block of text listing sources and are not functionally or contextually linked to a specific table for identification purposes. Additionally, it must not be a section heading or subheading that appears within the main body of text, as these serve to organize the document’s content rather than to label a specific table. Moreover, it must not be a heading for a subsection that is part of a larger narrative or argument, such as a section title like "3.3 The corpus", which serves to organize the document’s content rather than to label a specific table. Furthermore, it must not be a heading for a subsection that is part of a larger narrative or argument, such as a section title like "4.2.1 Participants" or "4.2.2 Task", which serve to organize the document’s content rather than to label a specific table. Additionally, it must not be a heading that is part of the table’s internal structure, such as a subheading within the table body that describes a group of rows or columns, as these are integral to the table’s data organization rather than serving as an external label for the entire table. Furthermore, it must not be an item in a table of contents, a list of figures, or a list of tables, as these are navigational structures that list document elements rather than labeling a specific table. It must also not be a heading for a chapter or section that is part of a larger document structure, such as "Chapter 7. Conclusions and Recommendations", as these serve to organize the document’s content rather than to label a specific table. Additionally, it must not be a title that appears at the top of a document or thesis cover page, as this serves as the main title of the work and is not associated with a specific table.'}]
table of contents text: [{'table of contents text': 'A structured, hierarchical index of document sections, presented as a navigational guide. It consists of a list of chapter and section titles, each paired with a corresponding page number, organized in a nested, multi-level structure using consistent indentation or numbering to denote hierarchy. The titles are aligned to the left, and page numbers are aligned to the right, separated by a dotted or dashed line. The content is exclusively textual, containing descriptive titles such as "Introduction" or "Methods," and is the sole content within its designated area, distinct from narrative text, figure captions, or standalone section headings found in the main body. It is typically found on a dedicated page at the beginning of a document, often preceded by a heading like "Contents" or "Table of Contents," and does not include mathematical equations, complex scientific notation, graphical elements, footnotes, endnotes, citations, bibliographic entries, or standalone page numbers. It must not be a simple, linear list of table or figure entries under a "LIST OF TABLES" or "LIST OF FIGURES" heading, nor should it contain paragraphs of continuous narrative text, data tables, or section headings embedded within the main body of the document. Furthermore, it must not contain entries that are descriptive captions or detailed explanations for figures or tables, which are typically presented as continuous, non-hierarchical sequences and are not part of the document’s structural navigation. It must not contain a single, standalone title that functions as the main document title, which is typically centered and appears on a title page. Additionally, it must not contain entries that are detailed, descriptive captions or explanations for figures or tables, which are typically presented as continuous, non-hierarchical sequences and are not part of the document’s structural navigation. Crucially, it must not contain entries that are descriptive, explanatory, or narrative text that functions as a figure or table caption, or that provides detailed methodology or results for a specific experiment or analysis, as such content is characteristic of the main body of the document and not part of the structural navigation index.'}]
title: [{'title': 'The primary, declarative heading of an academic dissertation, visually emphasized by its position at the top of the page, its large and bold typography, and its centered alignment, serving as the most prominent textual element that immediately identifies the document’s core subject matter. It is distinguished from secondary headings or lists by its maximal visual prominence and its role as the document’s overarching subject identifier, typically appearing before any other content or section headers, including navigational elements like tables of contents. Crucially, it is not a label for a table, figure, or other data presentation element, which are typically formatted with smaller, non-bold fonts and are positioned adjacent to or integrated within the data they describe. Furthermore, it is not a section heading within the document’s internal structure, such as a chapter or subsection heading, which are typically indented, numbered, or formatted with a different hierarchy and appear within the body of the text rather than at the top of the page.'}]
